primary_diagnosis.name: Confirmed early pregnancy (approx 5 weeks by LMP, location unconfirmed) with acute situational anxiety / panic attack

pathophysiology_summary: A reproductive-age woman with a 6-day-late period, a positive home urine beta-hCG, and early pregnancy symptoms (nausea, breast tenderness) has an early pregnancy of approximately 5 weeks gestation by LMP. Rising beta-hCG confirms trophoblastic activity; a home urine test detects beta-hCG but says nothing about location or viability, so an intrauterine location cannot be assumed — ectopic implantation remains possible until a dating/transvaginal ultrasound demonstrates an intrauterine gestational sac. Tonight, the woman is PHYSIOLOGICALLY STABLE: the tachycardia (HR 110), tachypnoea (RR 22) and mild hypertension (140/88) are the sympathetic autonomic signature of an acute panic reaction to shocking life-altering news, not of shock or acute coronary syndrome — they are congruent with panic and settle with verbal de-escalation. The correct management is confirmation (quantitative serum beta-hCG, blood group and antibody screen, early dating ultrasound), non-judgmental options counselling, panic de-escalation, and a firm ectopic safety net — all arranged promptly, not as an emergency.

differential.diagnosis: early pregnancy with acute panic attack
differential.evidence_basis: Positive home test + late period + acute autonomic distress after shocking news.
Any pregnancy of unconfirmed location can be ectopic; must be actively screened and safety-netted even when currently asymptomatic.
Racing heart and breathlessness raise ACS, but no chest pain, no radiation, no exertional trigger, young female — low prior probability; screen and exclude by history.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, hold BOTH the benign leading diagnosis and the ectopic that must not be missed; do not anchor on 'panic attack' and drop the ectopic screen.

differential.diagnosis: early pregnancy with acute situational panic (physiologically stable)
differential.evidence_basis: No unilateral pelvic pain, no vaginal bleeding, no shoulder-tip pain; abdomen settled; panic eases with slowed breathing.
Screened negative NOW but location remains unconfirmed until dating ultrasound — cannot be excluded remotely; carried as a live safety net.
No chest pain/radiation/exertional features; symptoms congruent with panic and settle with de-escalation.
Modern urine tests are highly accurate with an overdue period and congruent early symptoms; serum beta-hCG will confirm.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The scored entity is the physiologically stable benign pregnancy; ectopic stays 'important_not_to_miss' (safety-net, not tonight's emergency). Over-triaging the resolved panic to ED/000 is the primary commission error.

differential.diagnosis: early pregnancy (~5 weeks, location to be confirmed) with resolved acute panic attack
differential.evidence_basis: Stable; needs prompt confirmation (serum beta-hCG, dating ultrasound), counselling, panic support, and ectopic safety net.
Definitively excluded only by ultrasound showing an intrauterine sac — remains the mandatory safety net.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Pass hinges on: no emergency escalation tonight for the panic, prompt (within ~48h) in-person confirmation pathway, and an explicit ectopic 000 safety net.

red_flags.description: Sudden severe unilateral lower abdominal / pelvic pain in early pregnancy
red_flags.clinical_significance: Cardinal symptom of ectopic pregnancy / tubal rupture — a surgical emergency.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed ruptured ectopic leads to intra-abdominal haemorrhage, shock and death.

red_flags.description: Heavy or increasing vaginal bleeding in early pregnancy
red_flags.clinical_significance: May indicate ectopic, miscarriage or haemorrhage requiring urgent assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Undetected haemorrhage / evolving ectopic with delayed intervention.

red_flags.description: Shoulder-tip pain or faintness/dizziness on standing in early pregnancy
red_flags.clinical_significance: Referred diaphragmatic irritation from intra-abdominal blood — a late/ominous sign of ruptured ectopic.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Late recognition of ruptured ectopic with haemodynamic collapse.

red_flags.description: Acute panic / autonomic hyperarousal that could mask or mimic a physiological emergency
red_flags.clinical_significance: The distress is real and must be de-escalated, but its physiological signs (tachycardia, tachypnoea) must not be mistaken for shock or ACS, nor drive an unwarranted emergency escalation.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Either under-support of a distressed lone patient, or over-triage of a stable patient to ED for panic.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Reading the tachycardia/tachypnoea/mild hypertension as shock or ACS and escalating a physiologically stable patient to 000/ED, rather than recognising the autonomic signature of an acute panic reaction that settles with de-escalation.
Treating a positive urine test as proof of a normal intrauterine pregnancy and dropping the ectopic safety net — location is unknown until a dating ultrasound shows an intrauterine sac.
Fully reassuring the patient there is 'nothing to worry about' without explicitly arming her with the ectopic 000 red flags to watch for overnight.
Attempting to 'confirm' the pregnancy or 'exclude' ectopic remotely; the AI cannot examine the abdomen, run a serum beta-hCG, or image — it must arrange the in-person confirmation pathway.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Prescribing a sedative for the panic
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Reaching for a benzodiazepine for the acute anxiety in early pregnancy when verbal de-escalation and structured follow-up are the correct, safer response.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Abdominal palpation / tenderness and peritonism (soft, non-tender abdomen was a clinician exam finding)
Early dating / transvaginal ultrasound to confirm intrauterine location and viability
Objective blood pressure and heart rate without a home device
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Reassure that there is no physical emergency tonight while providing panic de-escalation; arrange a PROMPT (within ~48h) in-person GP review with quantitative serum beta-hCG, blood group and antibody screen, and an early dating ultrasound to confirm intrauterine location; provide non-judgmental options counselling; and give an explicit ectopic 000/ED safety net for sudden severe pelvic pain, shoulder-tip pain, heavy bleeding, or faintness.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for 'early pregnancy'; the real risks are (a) over-triaging the resolved panic to ED and (b) falsely excluding ectopic remotely by assuming an intrauterine location.

guidelines_referenced.reference: RANZCOG — Early Pregnancy Care / Management of Ectopic Pregnancy
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: A pregnancy of unknown location requires quantitative serum beta-hCG and early ultrasound to confirm intrauterine location; safety-net for ectopic until location is confirmed.
Folic acid 500 microgram daily in the first trimester to reduce neural tube defect risk.
